title: Salamanda: Dreams That Sound Like Reality
author: Allastair Voss
body:
Salamanda is the South Korean ambient duo of Sala (Jimin Sung) and Manda (Yejin Jang), who met in 2018 and have since built one of the more quietly imaginative catalogues in contemporary electronic music. Their album ashbalkum, released in 2022 on Brooklyn label Human Pitch, takes its title from a Korean phrase describing the moment you realize what you've been experiencing as reality is actually a dream. The whole record operates inside that ambiguity.

What makes Salamanda distinct in the wider ambient field is texture. They work with modular synths, field recordings, and what sounds like a deep archive of vintage video game soundtracks and Studio Ghibli scores. Tracks like Open Sesame and Catching Tails have a chime like clarity that feels less like a producer reaching for nostalgia and more like a producer reaching for memory itself. The sounds are familiar without being borrowed.

[spotify:artist/2ABEVwxsESCBsCBjN6qFru]

## Two Solo Practices, One Collaboration

Sala and Manda each have solo projects, Uman Therma and Yetsuby respectively, and the duo work feels like the place where their separate sensibilities resolve. Manda studied composition at university and brings a structural ear to the arrangements. Sala has no formal training and brings an instinctive openness to what a track can become. The combination produces music that's careful without being clean, where loose ends are part of the design.

The productive tension between trained structure and untrained intuition is audible throughout ashbalkum. The album doesn't develop in the way that composed music develops — building toward resolution, fulfilling harmonic promises, arriving at the place it telegraphed at the beginning. It drifts. It loops back. It moves sideways into textures that weren't prepared and then returns, or doesn't return. That is the Sala contribution, and it's what keeps the music from becoming academic.

Their 2023 album In Parallel pushed the project further into rhythm, leaning toward something closer to club music while keeping the dream logic of ashbalkum intact. It's the sort of move that risks losing the audience that came for ambient, but in their hands the shift reads as continuity rather than rupture. The kick drum that appears in In Parallel doesn't arrive as a genre pivot — it arrives as another texture, another material in the same ongoing investigation.

## What Ambient Does and What They Do Differently

Ambient music has a history that gets simplified into two competing models: Eno's music as furniture, designed to be heard and not listened to, and the more demanding strain that expects concentrated attention. Salamanda sit outside both traditions. Their music rewards attention without demanding it. You can have it on while doing other things and notice it differently each time — not because it's background music but because it's built from materials that behave differently depending on how closely you're paying attention.

The field recordings are an example of this. On the surface they function as texture, as color. Listen closer and they're specific: the particular reverb of a specific room, a sound that could be rain or could be something else, a recording grain that places you somewhere without naming the place. These are not production decisions made for prettiness. They're decisions about how the music creates a sense of being somewhere, of occupying a physical space that is also an emotional space.

This is what ashbalkum does with its title concept. The shift from reality to dream, or from dream to reality, is not announced. It's enacted. You don't realize you've crossed the threshold until you're already on the other side.

## The Ghibli Reference and What It Means

The Studio Ghibli comparison comes up often in writing about Salamanda and it's accurate enough but worth being specific about. What Ghibli scores do — and what composers like Joe Hisaishi achieved specifically — is music that is simultaneously present and distant, music that belongs to a world you are watching rather than a world you are in. The emotional effect is wistful without being sentimental, specific without being possessive.

Salamanda achieve something adjacent to this, but their version comes from electronics rather than orchestra and is rooted in the Korean musical and cultural context in ways that the Ghibli comparison doesn't fully accommodate. The chime sounds on ashbalkum have a specific timbral quality that connects to Korean percussion traditions as much as to any electronic antecedent. This is music with multiple inheritances, and the depth of the sound comes partly from the complexity of where it comes from.

## The Wider Listenership

Salamanda has played Boiler Room, Le Guess Who, and a steady run of European festivals, and their NTS guest mixes have become a reliable index of what's interesting in contemporary leftfield electronic. The Korean Music Awards nominated ashbalkum for best electronic album. The recognition matches the work.

The audience that has found Salamanda tends to be one that is already comfortable with music that doesn't declare its own significance. These are listeners who have stopped requiring that music tell them what to feel, who are interested in the stranger proposition of music that creates conditions for feeling without directing you toward a specific emotional destination. That audience is smaller than the mainstream. The music made for it is often better.

Salamanda are that kind of better. The catalogue they've built since 2018 is the work of two artists who found each other at the right moment and discovered that their differences were the source of the sound rather than an obstacle to it. That discovery is rare enough to be worth tracking wherever it goes next.
